stem: Hand operated switches in sidings may be considered lined for the normal position unless advised otherwise by…
choices: the RTC, GBO or special instruction
answer: the RTC, GBO or special instruction
explain: Hand operated switches in sidings may be considered lined for the normal position unless advised otherwise by the RTC, GBO or special instruction.

stem: A derail is identified in the field by…
choices: a reflective red letter “D” on a reflective yellow target (or a “Special Derail” sign)
answer: a reflective red letter “D” on a reflective yellow target (or a “Special Derail” sign)
explain: Derails are switch-stand operated and identified by a reflective red letter “D” on a reflective yellow target, or a sign indicating “Special Derail,” visible when in the derailing position.

stem: When equipment is left unattended, it must be…
choices: secured to prevent it from moving unintentionally
left with the air applied only
reported to the RTC within one hour
answer: secured to prevent it from moving unintentionally
explain: Rule 112: when equipment is left unattended it must be secured to prevent it from moving unintentionally.

stem: For securement purposes, equipment is “unattended” when…
choices: an employee is not in close enough proximity to take effective action
it is left for more than 30 minutes
answer: an employee is not in close enough proximity to take effective action
explain: UNATTENDED = when an employee is not in close enough proximity to take effective action (to stop the equipment).

stem: On tracks NOT listed in Rule 113.4, unless special instructions indicate otherwise, kicking of equipment is…
choices: permitted from the north end only
answer: prohibited
explain: Rule 113.5(a): on tracks not listed in Rule 113.4, unless otherwise indicated in special instructions, the kicking of equipment is prohibited.

stem: When equipment is shoved by an engine, a crew member must be…
choices: on the leading piece of equipment or on the ground, positioned to observe the track and give signals
on the rear of the engine
in the cab beside the engineer
answer: on the leading piece of equipment or on the ground, positioned to observe the track and give signals
explain: Rule 115: when shoving (or headed by an unmanned remote engine), a crew member must be on the leading piece of equipment or on the ground, in a position to observe the track to be used and give the signals/instructions to control the move (point protection).

stem: Before a running switch is commenced, what must be verified?
choices: the switch and hand brakes are in working order
the RTC has issued a clearance
answer: the switch and hand brakes are in working order
explain: Rule 113.6(a): it must be verified that the switch and hand brakes are in working order before the move is commenced.

stem: A running switch must NOT be made…
choices: with or onto occupied equipment, or equipment placarded for dangerous goods
answer: with or onto occupied equipment, or equipment placarded for dangerous goods
explain: Rule 113.6(b): a running switch must not be made with or onto occupied equipment, equipment placarded for dangerous goods, or where the switch to be used is not suitable.

stem: A movement must not foul a track until…
choices: the switches connected with the move are properly lined (or, for semi-automatic/spring switches, the conflicting route is known to be clear)
the air brakes are cut in
answer: the switches connected with the move are properly lined (or, for semi-automatic/spring switches, the conflicting route is known to be clear)
explain: Rule 114(b): a movement must not foul a track until the switches connected with the move are properly lined, or for semi-automatic/spring switches, the conflicting route is known to be clear. Equipment must not move foul of another track unless properly protected.

stem: In radio communication, “OVER” denotes…
choices: transmission is ended and a response is expected
transmission is ended and no response is expected
monitor this channel for my next transmission
answer: transmission is ended and a response is expected
explain: Rule 120(a): “OVER” = transmission is ended and a response is expected.

stem: In radio communication, “OUT” denotes…
choices: transmission is ended and no response is expected
transmission is ended and a response is expected
answer: transmission is ended and no response is expected
explain: Rule 120(a): “OUT” = transmission is ended and no response is expected.

stem: In radio communication, “STAND BY” denotes…
choices: monitor this channel for my next transmission
answer: monitor this channel for my next transmission
explain: Rule 120(a): “STAND BY” = monitor this channel for my next transmission.

stem: An initial radio call must commence with…
choices: the railway company initials of the person being called
answer: the railway company initials of the person being called
explain: Rule 121(a): the initial call must commence with the railway company initials of the person being called (positive identification).

stem: In switching by radio, direction must be given in relation to…
choices: the front of the controlling locomotive
answer: the front of the controlling locomotive
explain: Rule 123.2(i): direction in relation to the front of the controlling locomotive must be given in the initial instruction and whenever the direction changes.

stem: Safety-affecting radio instructions (e.g. car/distance counts in a back-up) must be…
answer: repeated back to the sender
explain: Verification (Rule 123): safety-affecting radio instructions must be repeated back to the sender — this is why the engineer repeats each car/distance count.

stem: Two staggered red lamps with NO plate indicate…
answer: Stop and Proceed (437)
explain: Two staggered reds with no plate = a non-controlled (automatic) signal: Stop and Proceed (437) — stop, then proceed at restricted speed.

stem: Which plates does the CROR actually use? (There is no “A” plate in Canada.)
answer: DV and R
explain: Only DV (diverging) and R (restricting) plates are used. An “A”/absolute plate is US practice and does not exist in the CROR.

stem: On a non-controlled signal, what is the tell between Restricting (436) and Stop and Proceed (437)?
choices: The R plate — same two staggered reds, but 436 carries an R plate and 437 does not
answer: The R plate — same two staggered reds, but 436 carries an R plate and 437 does not
explain: Two staggered reds with an R plate = Restricting (436); the same two staggered reds with no plate = Stop and Proceed (437).

stem: A single red on a controlled signal (one the RTC can hold at Stop) indicates…
answer: Stop (439) — absolute, no proceeding
explain: A single red on a controlled signal = Stop (439): an absolute stop with no proceeding (contrast the permissive two-staggered-reds of 437).

stem: Under conditions NOT provided for by the rules, crew members must…
choices: stop and call the RTC for instructions
do nothing until a rule applies
defer entirely to the locomotive engineer
answer: take every precaution for protection
explain: Rule 106: all crew members are responsible for safe operation and observance of the rules; under conditions not provided for by the rules, they must take every precaution for protection.

stem: When the locomotive engineer must temporarily leave the cab on a standing movement, they must…
choices: fully apply the independent brake (apply the automatic if required, and remove the reverser)
leave the throttle in idle only
answer: fully apply the independent brake (apply the automatic if required, and remove the reverser)
explain: Rule 109: when temporarily exiting the controlling cab on a standing movement, the LE must fully apply the independent brake, apply the automatic brake if required, and remove the reverser (unless otherwise provided).

stem: Unless special instructions say otherwise, the normal position of a main track hand operated switch is…
answer: for the main track route
explain: Normal is set for the main track route; main track switches must be left lined and locked in normal position.

stem: You must NOT turn a switch while any part of a car or engine is…
choices: between the switch points and the fouling point of the track to be used
answer: between the switch points and the fouling point of the track to be used
explain: Never throw a switch under equipment between the points and the fouling point (except a running switch or the Rule 114 exception) — it can derail equipment or split the switch.

stem: Except when switching, when passing over a main track switch, employees must keep at least how far from the switch stand?
answer: 20 feet
explain: Stay at least 20 feet from the switch stand (and on single track, on the opposite side when practicable).

stem: When using a crossover, which switch is reversed FIRST?
choices: the switch in the track the movement is standing on
answer: the switch in the track the movement is standing on
explain: Reverse the switch you're standing on first; both switches must be reversed before crossing, and the movement must be clear before either is restored.

stem: When a movement diverges from a track, the switch must not be restored to normal until…
choices: the fouling point has been cleared
answer: the fouling point has been cleared
explain: Don't restore the switch until the fouling point is cleared — restoring early can catch and derail the rear of the movement.

stem: Approaching a derail set in the derailing position, a movement must…
choices: proceed at restricted speed over it
answer: stop short of it
explain: A movement or track unit must stop short of a derail set in the derailing position.

stem: A derail (other than a Special or Blue Flag derail) must be left…
choices: in the derailing position, secured with a locking device
answer: in the derailing position, secured with a locking device
explain: Leave it in the derailing position and secured with a locking device — its whole job is to stop a runaway.

stem: Unless otherwise provided, movements on non-main track operate at REDUCED speed and must not exceed…
answer: 15 MPH
explain: Non-main track: REDUCED speed, not exceeding 15 MPH unless signal indication or special instructions allow more.

stem: A single piece of equipment left unattended must always be left with…
choices: the hand brake applied and tested for effectiveness
nothing, if the track is level
answer: the hand brake applied and tested for effectiveness
explain: A single piece of equipment must always be left with the hand brake applied and tested for effectiveness.

stem: You leave 8 cars (~1,800 trailing tons) unattended on a 1.0% grade. Per the Rule 112(g) table, the minimum hand brakes is…
explain: For 0–2000 trailing tons at a grade of ≤1.0%, the table requires 6 hand brakes — then test them for effectiveness.

stem: How is hand-brake effectiveness tested?
choices: with all air released, let the slack adjust or apply tractive effort to confirm it won't move
pull at full throttle and watch the brakes
count that enough brakes were applied
set the air and walk away
answer: with all air released, let the slack adjust or apply tractive effort to confirm it won't move
explain: With all air brakes released, either allow the slack to run in/out or apply tractive effort — it must be apparent the hand brakes alone hold the equipment.

stem: In a yard, before uncoupling from standing equipment, you must…
choices: apply a sufficient number of hand brakes (unless a Rule 112(c) method is used)
answer: apply a sufficient number of hand brakes (unless a Rule 112(c) method is used)
explain: Rule 113.1(b): before uncoupling from standing equipment in a yard, apply a sufficient number of hand brakes unless one of the Rule 112(c) methods is used — so it can't roll.

stem: Before coupling to equipment, Rule 113.0 requires that…
choices: at least one knuckle is open
answer: at least one knuckle is open
explain: Ensure at least one knuckle is open before coupling — otherwise you get a missed (by-pass) coupling.

stem: Coupling must be made at the lowest speed necessary and must not exceed…
answer: 6 MPH
explain: Couple at ≤6 MPH. (Coupling to passenger equipment is stricter — stop 6–12 ft out, then don't exceed 2 MPH.)

stem: After coupling, before moving, the equipment must be…
choices: stretched, then checked to confirm it is coupled and for any hand brakes normally expected
left to charge the air for 5 minutes
answer: stretched, then checked to confirm it is coupled and for any hand brakes normally expected
explain: Rule 113.2: equipment must be stretched; after stretching and before moving, check that it is coupled and for applied hand brakes that may normally be expected.

stem: Operative air brakes (besides the locomotives) must be used when switching on a grade greater than — and with more than — ?
explain: Air must be used when switching on a grade greater than 0.4% AND with more than 2000 tons.

stem: Kicking, running switch and gravity drop are flatly PROHIBITED on…
choices: a siding (also main track, subdivision track, high-risk locations, shop tracks, and onto/with passenger equipment)
answer: a siding (also main track, subdivision track, high-risk locations, shop tracks, and onto/with passenger equipment)
explain: Rule 113.4 prohibits them on main track, subdivision track, a siding, high-risk locations, shop tracks, and onto/with passenger equipment.

stem: What is the minimum number of employees required to perform a running switch?
explain: At least 3 employees must be used when performing a running switch.

stem: A hand or lantern signal swung from side to side, at right angle to the track, means…
answer: STOP
explain: Side to side at right angle to the track = STOP.

stem: A hand or lantern signal swung in a circle, at right angle to the track, means…
answer: move backward
explain: Swung in a circle at right angle to the track = MOVE BACKWARD (speed proportional to how fast it's swung).

stem: A hand or lantern signal raised and lowered means…
answer: move forward
explain: Raised and lowered (speed proportional) = MOVE FORWARD.

stem: A signal held horizontally at arm's length means…
answer: reduce speed
explain: Held horizontally at arm's length = REDUCE SPEED.

stem: If there is any doubt as to the meaning of a signal, or for whom it is intended, it must be regarded as…
answer: a STOP signal
explain: Doubt = STOP. The same principle applies on the radio (123.2(v)).

stem: While moving under hand signals, the signaller (or the signal light) disappears from view. This must be regarded as…
answer: a STOP signal
explain: Loss of sight of the signaller or the signal lights = STOP. Never keep moving blind.

stem: The person initiating a radio call ends the INITIAL call with the word…
answer: OVER
explain: The initiator ends the initial call with “OVER”; each party ends its FINAL transmission with “OUT” (121(c)).

stem: Switching by radio: you've travelled half the distance of the last instruction with no further communication. You must…
choices: repeat the call and keep moving
answer: STOP
explain: At half the distance of the last instruction with no further communication, STOP. Distance is given with every transmission.

stem: When car lengths are used to communicate distance on the radio, unless otherwise arranged, one car length equals…
choices: the actual length of that car
answer: 50 feet
explain: Unless otherwise arranged, a car length means 50 feet.

stem: A blue flag (and a blue light at night) displayed on equipment means…
choices: workmen are about the equipment — it must not be coupled to or moved until they remove the signal
the track is out of service
answer: workmen are about the equipment — it must not be coupled to or moved until they remove the signal
explain: Blue signal protection: workmen are about the equipment; it must not be coupled to or moved until those workmen remove the signal.

stem: A DV plate on a signal indicates the indication is upgraded to…
choices: DIVERGING (strip the DV plate and the same aspect downgrades to Slow)
answer: DIVERGING (strip the DV plate and the same aspect downgrades to Slow)
explain: A DV (diverging) plate upgrades the indication; remove it and the same lamp aspect downgrades to the Slow family.

stem: In the speed-signal system, a FLASHING green lamp denotes which speed?
answer: LIMITED (a steady green denotes Medium/Clear)
explain: Flashing green = LIMITED; steady green = Medium/Clear. The proceed lamp's position also encodes speed (green high = Clear, green low = Slow).

stem: A train or transfer must not pass a block signal indicating Stop unless…
choices: it has authority — the RTC may authorize it after ensuring there are no conflicting movements within or authorized to enter
the conductor is on the ground
answer: it has authority — the RTC may authorize it after ensuring there are no conflicting movements within or authorized to enter
explain: Rule 564: a train or transfer must have authority to pass a block signal indicating Stop; the RTC may authorize it, but first must ensure there are no conflicting trains/transfers within or authorized to enter.

stem: On a switch list, a car's tonnage and the track grade matter most directly for which rule?
answer: Securement — the Rule 112 hand-brake count
explain: Trailing tonnage + grade drive how many hand brakes you must apply when leaving the cut — read it off the list, find it on the 112(g) table.

stem: A car on your list is placarded for dangerous goods. Which move is prohibited onto it?
answer: A running switch
explain: No running switch may be made with or onto equipment placarded to indicate dangerous goods (kicking such equipment is restricted too) — handle it under control.

stem: You're shoving a long cut and you can personally see the whole track is clear and nobody's fouling it. Does Rule 115 still require a crew member on the point or on the ground?
choices: No — point protection isn't required when the track is known to be clear; otherwise it is
Only if the shove is over 10 car lengths
answer: No — point protection isn't required when the track is known to be clear; otherwise it is
explain: Rule 115 needs a crew member positioned to observe and control the shove — EXCEPT where the track is known to be clear. 'Known to be clear' is a high bar, not a convenience.

stem: You've just coupled onto a cut and the engineer is ready to pull. Before the equipment moves, what does Rule 113.2 require?
choices: Stretch the equipment, then check it's coupled and for applied hand brakes
Nothing — a completed coupling is enough
answer: Stretch the equipment, then check it's coupled and for applied hand brakes
explain: Rule 113.2: equipment must be stretched, and after stretching and before moving it must be checked (i) that it's coupled and (ii) for applied hand brakes. The stretch proves the joint.

stem: The track is lined for your shove, but the conflicting switch into the next track isn't lined yet. Can you let your movement foul that track?
choices: No — don't foul a track until the switches connected with the move are properly lined
Yes, that's the other crew's track
answer: No — don't foul a track until the switches connected with the move are properly lined
explain: Rule 114(b): a movement must not foul a track until the switches connected with the move are properly lined (or, for semi-automatic/spring switches, the conflicting route is known to be clear).

stem: You're set to kick cars in, but there's debris on the walking surface where you'd pull the pin. What does Rule 113.5 require first?
choices: The walking surface where equipment is uncoupled must be clear of obstacles
Nothing — footing is the conductor's own lookout
Two hand brakes on the cut
A blue flag at the lead
answer: The walking surface where equipment is uncoupled must be clear of obstacles
explain: Rule 113.5: where kicking is permitted, the walking surface of the area where equipment is uncoupled must be clear of obstacles — you're stepping and pulling pins in there.

stem: You're coupling onto standing passenger equipment. How does that differ from coupling to freight?
choices: Stop 6–12 feet out, then close at no more than 2 MPH
Same as freight — 6 MPH is fine
No limit if the air is cut out
Couple only from the south end
answer: Stop 6–12 feet out, then close at no more than 2 MPH
explain: Coupling is made at the lowest speed necessary, not exceeding 6 MPH — but to passenger equipment you stop 6–12 ft away first, then close at no more than 2 MPH.

stem: There are cars standing on the track you meant to make a running switch onto. Can you make it?
choices: No — a running switch must not be made with or onto occupied equipment
Yes, if the standing cars are tied down
answer: No — a running switch must not be made with or onto occupied equipment
explain: Rule 113.6(b): a running switch must not be made with or onto occupied equipment (nor onto dangerous-goods equipment, nor where the switch can't be safely restored).

stem: In OCS territory, a train or transfer is authorized to occupy the track by…
choices: a clearance issued by the RTC
entering when the track looks clear
answer: a clearance issued by the RTC
explain: OCS has no wayside block signals — authority to foul or enter the track comes from a clearance issued by the RTC.

stem: OCS territory is supervised by the RTC by means of…
choices: clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions
answer: clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions
explain: Rule 301: the RTC supervises OCS territory by means of clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions as required.

stem: Except within cautionary limits, what must a train/transfer have before it fouls or enters OCS track?
answer: a clearance
explain: Rule 302(a): except within cautionary limits, a train or transfer must be authorized by a clearance to foul or enter a track where OCS rules apply.

stem: In multi-track OCS, a clearance must…
choices: specify the track(s) to be used
be copied by one crew member
answer: specify the track(s) to be used
explain: Rule 306: in multi-track OCS, a clearance must specify the track(s) to be used.

stem: A work clearance permits a train or transfer to…
choices: move in either direction within the limits named, until it is superseded or cancelled
answer: move in either direction within the limits named, until it is superseded or cancelled
explain: Rule 308: a work clearance lets a train/transfer move in either direction within the named limits and remains in effect until superseded or cancelled.

stem: Required to stop at a meeting, clearing or waiting point, a train must be stopped…
choices: clear of the route to be used by another train or transfer
at least 300 feet short of the point
answer: clear of the route to be used by another train or transfer
explain: Rule 304.1: stop clear of the route to be used by another train or transfer (stopping clear of the fouling point).

stem: Restricted by clearance, a train must not leave the named point until…
choices: it is positively known the opposing train(s)/transfer(s) named on the clearance have arrived
answer: it is positively known the opposing train(s)/transfer(s) named on the clearance have arrived
explain: Rule 304: do not leave the point named until it is positively known the opposing train(s)/transfer(s) named on the clearance have arrived.

stem: Under Rule 304, an opposing train has NOT 'arrived' until…
choices: its designated engine AND marker have arrived
its head end passes the point
answer: its designated engine AND marker have arrived
explain: A train or transfer has not arrived until its designated engine and marker have arrived — the marker proves the whole train is in.

stem: Before issuing clearance authority, the RTC must…
choices: provide protection against all conflicting movements
wait for the previous clearance to be cancelled
answer: provide protection against all conflicting movements
explain: Rule 305: before issuing clearance authority, the RTC must provide protection against all conflicting movements.

stem: In ABS, block signals govern the use of the blocks but do NOT…
answer: authorize main track occupancy
explain: Rule 505: block signals govern use of the blocks; they do not dispense with other signals and do not authorize main track occupancy.

stem: When ABS signals are withdrawn from service, movements are governed by…
choices: instructions from the RTC or special instructions
answer: instructions from the RTC or special instructions
explain: Rule 507: when ABS signals are withdrawn from service, movements are governed by instructions from the RTC or special instructions.

stem: To pass an ABS block signal indicating Stop, a movement must have…
choices: two crew members on the ground
nothing if no train is in sight
answer: instructions from the RTC
explain: Rule 509(a): a movement must have instructions from the RTC to pass a block signal indicating Stop.

stem: Stopped at an ABS signal indicating Stop with no conflicting movement evident, a crew member must…
answer: immediately communicate with the RTC
explain: Rule 509(a): if stopped at the signal indicating Stop and no conflicting movement is evident, a crew member must immediately communicate with the RTC.

stem: Entering/fouling a main track in ABS with no movement observed approaching, a crew member must reverse the switch and…
choices: wait five minutes (unless a greater period is specified), remaining at the switch
call the RTC for a clearance
answer: wait five minutes (unless a greater period is specified), remaining at the switch
explain: Rule 513(a): reverse the switch and wait five minutes (unless a greater period is specified) before moving foul of the main track; the crew member must remain at the switch.

stem: A movement that entered a block on a signal permitting better than RESTRICTED speed, then is stopped/delayed in the block, must move to the next signal at…
choices: REDUCED speed (unless no switches between, or until the track is seen to be clear)
the speed of the original signal
answer: REDUCED speed (unless no switches between, or until the track is seen to be clear)
explain: Rule 515: if delayed in the block, move at REDUCED speed to the next signal — unless there are no switches between, or until the track is seen to be clear.

stem: In CTC, the operation of trains and transfers is governed by…
choices: block signals, supervised by the RTC
answer: block signals, supervised by the RTC
explain: Rule 560: CTC is supervised by the RTC and block signals govern the operation of trains or transfers.

stem: When all or part of CTC is withdrawn from service, trains and transfers are governed by…
answer: special instructions
explain: Rule 561: when CTC is withdrawn from service, trains and transfers are governed by special instructions.

stem: To pass a CTC block signal indicating Stop, a train or transfer must…
choices: have authority — the RTC may authorize it after ensuring no conflicting movements
reduce to restricted speed and proceed
get a hand signal from the conductor
answer: have authority — the RTC may authorize it after ensuring no conflicting movements
explain: Rule 564: a train/transfer must have authority to pass a block signal indicating Stop; the RTC may authorize it only after ensuring no conflicting movements are within, or authorized to enter, the limits.

stem: A CTC work authority permits a train or transfer to…
choices: move in either direction within specified limits
answer: move in either direction within specified limits
explain: Rule 566: work authority permits moving in either direction within the specified limits.

stem: Before issuing CTC work authority, the RTC must…
choices: ensure no other trains are within/authorized to enter, AND block at Stop all controlling devices
answer: ensure no other trains are within/authorized to enter, AND block at Stop all controlling devices
explain: Rule 566: before issuing work authority, the RTC must ensure there are no other trains/transfers within or authorized to enter the limits, and block at Stop all devices controlling signals into those limits.

stem: A train leaving CTC and entering ABS, if it must pass a signal indicating Stop, is governed by…
choices: Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS
answer: Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS
explain: Rule 565: such a movement is governed by Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS.

stem: Within interlocking limits, the use of the routes is governed by…
answer: interlocking signal indications
explain: Rule 601: within interlocking limits a movement is governed by interlocking rules, and interlocking signal indications govern the use of the routes (a signalman may issue instructions when necessary).

stem: Except in an emergency, radio or hand signals must NOT be used when…
choices: the proper indication can be displayed by the interlocking signals
answer: the proper indication can be displayed by the interlocking signals
explain: Rule 602(a): except in case of emergency, radio or hand signals must not be used when the proper indication can be displayed by the interlocking signals.

stem: At a location not protected by an advance signal, a movement must approach interlocking limits…
choices: prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop
answer: prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop
explain: Rule 606: where there is no advance signal, approach interlocking limits prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop.

stem: When an interlocking signal indicates Stop (no conflicting movement evident), which rule applies depends on…
choices: the type of interlocking: Manual=608, Locally-Controlled=609, Remotely-Controlled=610, Automatic=611
answer: the type of interlocking: Manual=608, Locally-Controlled=609, Remotely-Controlled=610, Automatic=611
explain: Rule 607: the applicable rule depends on the interlocking type (per special instructions) — Manual 608, Locally-Controlled 609, Remotely-Controlled 610, Automatic 611.

stem: When an interlocking is located in ABS or CTC, the indication of the LAST interlocking signal in the direction of travel…
choices: also governs the movement to the next signal or Block End sign
stops governing once limits are cleared
answer: also governs the movement to the next signal or Block End sign
explain: Rule 614: when an interlocking is in ABS or CTC, the last interlocking signal's indication also governs the movement to the next signal or Block End sign.

stem: General Rule C(ii): at all times you must expect…
choices: a movement, track unit or equipment to move at any time, on any track, in either direction
equipment to stay put unless you're told otherwise
trains only on the main track
answer: a movement, track unit or equipment to move at any time, on any track, in either direction
explain: The foundational safety mindset: expect movement at any time, on any track, in either direction. Never assume a track is dead.

stem: A reliable watch carried on duty must indicate…
answer: hours, minutes and seconds
explain: Rule 2 (Watches): conductors, LEs, foremen, etc. must use a reliable watch indicating hours, minutes and seconds and keep it correct.

stem: Sleeping (or assuming the position of sleeping) and personal entertainment devices on duty are…
choices: allowed if the train is stopped
answer: prohibited
explain: General Rule A(xi): sleeping or assuming the position of sleeping is prohibited, as is the use of personal entertainment devices.

stem: When reporting for duty, General Rule A requires an employee to be…
choices: fit, rested, and familiar with their duties and territory
answer: fit, rested, and familiar with their duties and territory
explain: General Rule A(x): when reporting for duty, be fit, rested and familiar with your duties and the territory over which you operate.

stem: In the operating cab, cellular telephones…
choices: must not be used when normal railway radio communications are available
answer: must not be used when normal railway radio communications are available
explain: General Rule A(xii): restrict communication devices to railway matters; cell phones must not be used when normal railway radio is available, and if used in lieu of radio all radio rules apply.

stem: You must not entrain or detrain MOVING equipment at a speed exceeding…
choices: walking speed of the slowest crew member
answer: 4 MPH (except in an emergency)
explain: General Rule C(viii): do not get on or off moving equipment above 4 MPH except in an emergency, and the intent must be communicated.

stem: Being on the END of a car while it is in motion is prohibited except…
choices: for the purpose of operating a hand brake
when riding to the next switch
answer: for the purpose of operating a hand brake
explain: General Rule C(vi): don't be on the end of a moving car except to operate a hand brake.

stem: How often must you pass the rules examination (recertification)?
choices: At prescribed intervals not exceeding three years, carrying a valid certificate
answer: At prescribed intervals not exceeding three years, carrying a valid certificate
explain: General Rule A(vii): pass the required examination at intervals not exceeding three years and carry a valid certificate of rules qualification while on duty.

stem: If you are in doubt as to the meaning of a rule or instruction, you must…
choices: seek clarification from the proper authority
follow the most cautious option silently
answer: seek clarification from the proper authority
explain: General Rule A(viii): seek clarification from the proper authority if in doubt — and A(iii) requires reporting violations promptly.

stem: At a crossing with automatic warning devices NOT equipped with gates, they must be observed operating for a minimum of…
choices: 20 seconds (unless a greater time is prescribed)
answer: 20 seconds (unless a greater time is prescribed)
explain: Rule 103.1(a)(i): minimum required time at a gateless crossing is 20 seconds, unless special instructions prescribe more.

stem: At a GATED crossing where the maximum allowable speed is GREATER than 15 mph, the gates must be observed horizontal for a minimum of…
answer: 5 seconds
explain: Rule 103.1(a)(ii): above 15 mph, the gates must be observed in the horizontal position for at least 5 seconds before occupying the crossing.

stem: At a GATED crossing where the maximum allowable speed is 15 mph or less, before occupying it you must observe…
choices: the gates in the horizontal position
answer: the gates in the horizontal position
explain: Rule 103.1(a)(ii): at 15 mph or less, the gates simply must be observed in the horizontal position (no separate seconds count).

stem: The 'Minimum Required Time' for warning devices applies unless…
choices: manual protection of the crossing is provided
the crossing is in a yard
answer: manual protection of the crossing is provided
explain: Rule 103.1(a): the minimum-required-time observation applies unless manual protection is provided at the crossing.

stem: Gateless crossing needs 20 s; a gated crossing at >15 mph needs the gates horizontal for…
answer: 5 seconds
explain: Don't conflate them: gateless = 20 s of operation; gated above 15 mph = 5 s of gates horizontal; gated at ≤15 mph = simply observe the gates horizontal.

stem: Who maintains the indelible written record of each GBO, clearance, TOP and authority?
answer: the RTC
explain: Rule 131: the RTC must maintain a complete indelible record (book or computer-assisted system) of each GBO, clearance, TOP, authority and instruction required to be in writing.

stem: The body of each GBO must be given…
choices: in the same words and figures to each employee and movement addressed
answer: in the same words and figures to each employee and movement addressed
explain: Rule 151 (Identical Meaning to All): the body of each GBO must be given in the same words and figures to everyone addressed.

stem: When copying a GBO/clearance/TOP from the RTC, you must…
choices: copy as it is transmitted and repeat from the copy received all applicable written and pre-printed portions
copy only the parts that apply to you
record it after the call ends
answer: copy as it is transmitted and repeat from the copy received all applicable written and pre-printed portions
explain: Rule 136(a): copy as transmitted and repeat back from the copy received all applicable written and pre-printed portions (spelling out station names).

stem: A GBO, clearance or authority and its record must…
choices: contain only essential information — brief but clear, in the prescribed form, without erasure
include the RTC's name in the body
answer: contain only essential information — brief but clear, in the prescribed form, without erasure
explain: Rule 132 (Brevity, Clarity…): only essential information, brief but clear, in the prescribed form, without erasure or anything that makes it hard to read.

stem: A GBO or other authority is addressed to…
choices: those who are to execute and observe it, clearly and leaving no doubt
answer: those who are to execute and observe it, clearly and leaving no doubt
explain: Rule 134 (Designation of Movements): authorities are addressed clearly and concisely to those who must execute and observe them.

stem: The RTC must ensure that movements affected by a GBO…
choices: are issued a copy of the GBO, or are otherwise secured
hear it on the radio once
answer: are issued a copy of the GBO, or are otherwise secured
explain: Rule 152 (Delivery of GBO): the RTC must ensure affected movements receive a copy or are otherwise secured.

stem: Confirmation of protection must NOT be given to a foreman until…
choices: all movements affected have received a copy of the GBO or are otherwise secured
answer: all movements affected have received a copy of the GBO or are otherwise secured
explain: Rule 153: don't confirm protection to a foreman until all affected movements have a copy of the GBO or are otherwise secured — that's what makes the limits safe.

stem: Who is the employee in charge of the protection of track work and track units?
answer: the foreman
explain: By definition the foreman is the employee in charge of the protection of track work and track units.

stem: Under Planned Protection (Rule 42), the request for protection must be…
choices: in writing and on the prescribed form
answer: in writing and on the prescribed form
explain: Rule 42(a): when protection is required, the request must be in writing and on the prescribed form.

stem: Before a foreman arranges to display the prescribed flags, the track and time limits must be…
answer: confirmed in writing
explain: Rule 42(a): once protection has been provided, the track and time limits must be confirmed in writing before the foreman arranges for the flags.

stem: Special instructions specify when Rules 42/842 and 43/843 are applicable on…
answer: non-main track
explain: Rule 40(a): special instructions specify when Rules 42/842, 43/843 and 849 are applicable on non-main track.

stem: Restricting (Rule 436) means…
answer: proceed at RESTRICTED speed
explain: Restricting (436): proceed at RESTRICTED speed — prepared to stop within half the range of vision, short of equipment, a switch not properly lined, etc.

stem: Two staggered red lamps with NO number/marker plate read as…
choices: Stop and Proceed (437) — stop, then proceed at restricted speed
answer: Stop and Proceed (437) — stop, then proceed at restricted speed
explain: Two staggered reds, no plate = a non-controlled (automatic) signal: Stop and Proceed. The same two staggered reds WITH an R plate read as Restricting (436).

stem: How do you tell an ABSOLUTE Stop (439) from a permissive Stop and Proceed (437)?
choices: 439 is a controlled signal the RTC holds at Stop — no proceeding without authority; 437 shows the two staggered reds of an automatic signal
answer: 439 is a controlled signal the RTC holds at Stop — no proceeding without authority; 437 shows the two staggered reds of an automatic signal
explain: The tell is controlled vs non-controlled: an absolute Stop (439) is held by the RTC and you don't pass it without authority; 437's staggered reds mark an automatic signal you may stop-then-proceed past at restricted speed.

stem: Which marker plates are used in the CROR — and which does NOT exist in Canada?
choices: DV and R (and L for Limited) are used; there is NO 'A'/absolute plate
A and R are used; no DV
answer: DV and R (and L for Limited) are used; there is NO 'A'/absolute plate
explain: DV and R plates (and an L marker for Limited) are real; the 'A'/absolute plate is US practice and does NOT exist in the CROR.

stem: A Diverging indication carries a DV plate. Strip the DV plate off the same aspect and it becomes…
answer: Slow
explain: The DV plate is the upgrade: it raises Slow to Diverging. Remove it and the aspect downgrades to the Slow family.

stem: In the turnout/diverging family (Limited / Medium / Diverging / Slow to ___), the FIRST word tells you…
choices: the speed through the signal and turnouts
how many blocks are clear ahead
answer: the speed through the signal and turnouts
explain: For the turnout family the first word IS a real speed (Limited 45 / Medium 30 / Diverging 25 / Slow 15) governing how you pass the signal and its turnouts; the second word is the next signal's indication.

stem: The straight-route block signals (Clear, Clear to Stop, Advance Clear to Stop…) primarily tell you…
choices: how many blocks ahead are clear before an occupancy — how much warning you have
the speed to run past the signal
answer: how many blocks ahead are clear before an occupancy — how much warning you have
explain: Straight-route aspects are about block warning, not 'speed past the signal' — speed only governs the turnout/diverging family.

stem: In an aspect, a FLASHING green lamp signifies…
answer: LIMITED
explain: Flashing green = LIMITED; steady green = Medium/Clear. The flash is the tell that separates the Limited family from the Medium family.

stem: You approach a signal showing yellow over red — Clear to Stop (411). What is it telling you?
choices: Your block is clear but the next is occupied — proceed prepared to stop at the next signal
answer: Your block is clear but the next is occupied — proceed prepared to stop at the next signal
explain: Clear to Stop (411): proceed, preparing to stop at the next signal — your block is clear, the next is not.

stem: A FLASHING yellow over red (Advance Clear to Stop, 415) differs from plain Clear to Stop (411) how?
choices: One more block of warning — your block AND the next are clear, the third is occupied; prepare to stop at the SECOND signal
answer: One more block of warning — your block AND the next are clear, the third is occupied; prepare to stop at the SECOND signal
explain: The flash adds a block of advance warning: 415 prepares you to stop at the second signal, not the first.

stem: A single green (Clear, Rule 405) indicates…
answer: Proceed
explain: Clear (405): Proceed. A single green — two greens is Clear to Medium (407), not Clear.

stem: 'Set and center' is…
choices: CN operating practice (GOI), not a numbered CROR rule — nearest is Rule 109
answer: CN operating practice (GOI), not a numbered CROR rule — nearest is Rule 109
explain: 'Set and center' is CN operating practice (GOI), not CROR. Always distinguish CROR rules from CN operating instructions; the nearest CROR rule is 109.

stem: On the radio, 'OUT' means…
answer: transmission ended, NO response expected
explain: Rule 120(a): 'OUT' = transmission ended and no response expected; 'OVER' = ended and a response IS expected; 'STAND BY' = monitor for my next transmission.

stem: Radio communications must be…
choices: brief and to the point — only essential instructions or information
answer: brief and to the point — only essential instructions or information
explain: Rule 122: keep it brief and to the point, containing only essential instructions or information.

stem: A radio call commences with…
choices: the railway initials of the party being called
answer: the railway initials of the party being called
explain: Rule 121(a): the initial call commences with the railway company initials of the party being called; both parties establish positive identification.

stem: During routine switching by radio, why don't the running count calls each end with 'over'?
choices: Rule 120(b) — the switching exception to the 'over' requirement
because the engine number replaces it
answer: Rule 120(b) — the switching exception to the 'over' requirement
explain: Rule 120(b): routine switching communications are excepted from each call carrying 'over' — it keeps the count quick.

stem: Switching by radio, you're unsure what an instruction meant or whether it was for your movement. You must…
choices: treat the doubt as a stop — do not act until it's clarified
assume it was for you and proceed
keep moving at the same speed
ask the RTC to repeat after you finish the move
answer: treat the doubt as a stop — do not act until it's clarified
explain: Rule 123.2(v): if there is doubt as to meaning or that the communication is for your movement, stop. Doubt is always a stop, radio or hand signal.

stem: If you witness a violation of the rules, General Rule A requires you to…
choices: report it promptly to the proper authority
note it at end of shift
answer: report it promptly to the proper authority
explain: General Rule A(iii): provide every possible assistance to ensure compliance, and report any violations promptly to the proper authority.

stem: You spot a condition that could affect the safe operation of a movement. General Rule A says…
choices: communicate it by the quickest available means to the proper authority, and get assistance promptly if needed to control it
wait and see if it worsens
only report it if it's on your track
answer: communicate it by the quickest available means to the proper authority, and get assistance promptly if needed to control it
explain: General Rule A(iv)/(v): report safety-affecting conditions by the quickest means, and obtain assistance promptly when needed to control a harmful or dangerous condition.

stem: While on duty you must have accessible…
choices: the rule book, GOI, current timetable and supplements, and other required documents
answer: the rule book, GOI, current timetable and supplements, and other required documents
explain: General Rule A(ii): have a copy of the rule book, the general operating instructions, current timetable and supplements, and other specified documents accessible while on duty.

stem: General Rule C(i) requires every employee to…
choices: be vigilant to avoid the risk of injury to themselves or others
answer: be vigilant to avoid the risk of injury to themselves or others
explain: General Rule C(i): be vigilant to avoid the risk of injury to yourself or others.

stem: To board a slowly moving cut, can you step in front of it to entrain?
choices: No — never stand in front of approaching equipment to entrain
answer: No — never stand in front of approaching equipment to entrain
explain: General Rule C(iii): do not stand in front of approaching equipment for the purpose of entraining.

stem: Riding a moving open-top car, may you ride on the lading?
choices: No — not on the roof of moving equipment, nor on the lading of a moving open-top car
answer: No — not on the roof of moving equipment, nor on the lading of a moving open-top car
explain: General Rule C(v): don't be on the roof of moving equipment or on the lading of a moving open-top car.

stem: A car has a known shifted load. May you ride it or the car directly behind it?
choices: No — don't ride a car (or the car trailing it) known or suspected to have a shifted or damaged load
Yes, the trailing car is fine
Yes, if you ride the far end
answer: No — don't ride a car (or the car trailing it) known or suspected to have a shifted or damaged load
explain: General Rule C(vii): don't ride any car known/suspected to contain a shifted load or damage that may compromise its structure — or any car trailing such a car.

stem: Special instructions are found in…
choices: timetables, GOI, operating bulletins, or GBO
answer: timetables, GOI, operating bulletins, or GBO
explain: General Rule B: special instructions appear in timetables, general operating instructions, operating bulletins, or GBO.

stem: A new timetable or supplement is posted by operating bulletin at least how long before it takes effect?
choices: 72 hours (and by GBO at least 24 hours before)
answer: 72 hours (and by GBO at least 24 hours before)
explain: Notice of a new timetable/supplement is posted by operating bulletin at least 72 hours prior, and given by GBO at least 24 hours prior.

stem: Beyond the CROR, every employee must also be governed by…
choices: every safety rule and instruction of the company pertaining to their occupation
answer: every safety rule and instruction of the company pertaining to their occupation
explain: General Rule A(vi): be conversant with and governed by every safety rule and instruction of the company pertaining to your occupation.

stem: Riding the side of a car, you approach a tight side or overhead clearance. General Rule C(iv) requires you to…
choices: not ride the side or above the roof of moving equipment when passing side and/or overhead restrictions
get off only if it looks close
answer: not ride the side or above the roof of moving equipment when passing side and/or overhead restrictions
explain: General Rule C(iv): don't ride the side or above the roof of moving equipment when passing side and/or overhead restrictions — clearances can be unforgiving.

stem: Trailing through a spring switch, your movement stops partway through it. Before reversing or taking slack, you must…
choices: wait until the switch has been properly set by hand
take slack to re-align the points
answer: wait until the switch has been properly set by hand
explain: Rule 104.1(c): trailing through a spring switch, a movement that stops must not be reversed, nor slack taken, until the switch has been properly set by hand — otherwise you can split it.

stem: A spring switch is identified in the field by…
choices: a red 'D' on a yellow target
answer: a sign bearing the letters 'SS'
explain: Rule 104.1(a): a spring switch is identified by a spring-switch sign bearing the letters 'SS'.

stem: A spring switch handle is being lifted or released. Where must employees be?
answer: clear of the switch handle
explain: Rule 104.1(b): keep clear of the switch handle while it is being lifted or released — it can snap.

stem: Authorized to pass a stop signal to move over a power-operated switch, a crew member must…
choices: observe that the switch points are lined for the authorized route
throw the switch to hand operation first
answer: observe that the switch points are lined for the authorized route
explain: Rule 104.3: a crew member must observe that the points are lined for the authorized route before the movement proceeds over a power-operated switch at a stop signal.

stem: A semi-automatic switch is equipped with…
answer: reflectorized targets
explain: Rule 104.4(a): a semi-automatic switch is equipped with reflectorized targets.

stem: Ice or snow may keep a semi-automatic switch's points from closing on the wheel flange. Before a trailing move, a crew member must…
choices: manually line the switch and ensure the points are properly lined
proceed slowly and hope they close
answer: manually line the switch and ensure the points are properly lined
explain: Rule 104.4(b): in ice/snow, manually line the switch and ensure the points are properly lined before a trailing move — don't trust the flange to close them.

stem: Unless advised otherwise by the RTC, GBO, or special instruction, hand-operated switches in sidings may be considered…
answer: lined for the normal position
explain: Rule 104: hand-operated switches in sidings may be considered lined for the normal position unless advised otherwise — but verify, because 'considered' is not 'confirmed.'

stem: You've crossed over on a crossover. When may the switches be restored to normal?
choices: only after the movement is clear — and both must have been reversed before crossing
right after the head end passes the first switch
one at a time as you go
answer: only after the movement is clear — and both must have been reversed before crossing
explain: Rule 104(n): both crossover switches must be reversed before crossing, and the movement must be clear before either is restored — restoring early can catch the rear of your move.

stem: A derail (other than a Special or Blue Flag derail) is identified in the field by…
choices: a reflective red 'D' on a reflective yellow target
answer: a reflective red 'D' on a reflective yellow target
explain: Rule 104.5: a derail is switch-stand operated and identified by a reflective red letter 'D' on a reflective yellow target (or a 'Special Derail' sign), visible when in the derailing position.

stem: Before leaving equipment you've secured, Rule 112 requires you to…
choices: confirm with another employee the manner in which it has been secured
release the hand brakes once the air sets
answer: confirm with another employee the manner in which it has been secured
explain: Rule 112(iv): before leaving, the employee securing the equipment must confirm with another employee the manner in which it has been secured.

stem: Your hand-brake effectiveness test shows the equipment can still move. You must…
choices: apply one or more additional hand brakes and re-test
leave it — the air will hold it
apply the locomotive independent brake instead
answer: apply one or more additional hand brakes and re-test
explain: Rule 112(vi): if the hand brakes aren't sufficient, apply one or more additional hand brakes and re-test until it holds.

stem: Under Rule 112, parking brakes are…
choices: a substitute for the air test
answer: considered to be hand brakes
explain: Rule 112(ii): parking brakes are considered to be hand brakes.

stem: Hand brakes for securement must NOT be applied…
choices: while the equipment is being pulled or shoved
answer: while the equipment is being pulled or shoved
explain: Rule 112(iii): application of hand brakes must not be made while equipment is being pulled or shoved — stop it first.

stem: You're leaving a cut with locomotives coupled and unattended. The locomotive hand brakes…
choices: must be applied on all locomotives in the lead consist, and are NOT counted toward the number required on the cars
aren't needed if the cars are braked
answer: must be applied on all locomotives in the lead consist, and are NOT counted toward the number required on the cars
explain: Rule 112(v): apply hand brakes on all locomotives in the lead consist — and don't count them when determining how many the cars need.

stem: On a siding, when is equipment NOT considered unattended (so the hand-brake securement isn't required)?
choices: coupled to a controlling locomotive, brake pipe coupled and open, with a qualified employee aboard able to operate the air brakes
when the air brakes are simply applied
when it's been there under an hour
answer: coupled to a controlling locomotive, brake pipe coupled and open, with a qualified employee aboard able to operate the air brakes
explain: Rule 112(a): on Main/Subdivision/Siding/High-Risk, equipment is unattended and must be secured UNLESS coupled to a controlling loco with the brake pipe coupled and open and a qualified employee aboard able to work the air.

stem: Rule 112(a)'s controlling-locomotive exception can also be met when…
choices: a locomotive engineer is on the ground per Rule 109, within arm's reach of the locomotive, completing passing-train inspections
answer: a locomotive engineer is on the ground per Rule 109, within arm's reach of the locomotive, completing passing-train inspections
explain: Rule 112(a): alternatively a locomotive engineer may be on the ground per CROR 109, within arm's reach of the locomotive, to complete passing train/transfer inspections.

stem: Rule 112(a)'s securement requirements apply on…
choices: Main Track, Subdivision Track, a Siding, or High Risk Locations
answer: Main Track, Subdivision Track, a Siding, or High Risk Locations
explain: Rule 112(a) governs unattended equipment on Main Track, Subdivision Track, a Siding, or High Risk Locations.

stem: Leaving a cut unattended, can the air brakes alone be relied on to hold it?
choices: No — air isn't securement; apply the required hand brakes and test for effectiveness
Yes, with the angle cock closed
answer: No — air isn't securement; apply the required hand brakes and test for effectiveness
explain: Air brakes leak off — they are not securement. Rule 112 securement means hand brakes (parking brakes count), applied and tested for effectiveness.

stem: Same cut, but now you're leaving it on a steeper grade. What changes about securing it?
choices: More hand brakes are required (the Rule 112 chart scales with grade and tonnage) — and you still test for effectiveness
You can leave it for the next crew
answer: More hand brakes are required (the Rule 112 chart scales with grade and tonnage) — and you still test for effectiveness
explain: The Rule 112 hand-brake chart scales with average grade and total trailing tons — steeper/heavier needs more — and you always finish by testing effectiveness.

stem: Under Rule 113.1, equipment is considered uncoupled once…
choices: the uncoupling lever has been lifted
answer: the uncoupling lever has been lifted
explain: Rule 113.1(a): equipment is considered uncoupled once the uncoupling lever has been lifted.

stem: Which rule governs securing equipment after an emergency brake application on a grade?
answer: Rule 66
explain: Rule 66 — Securing Equipment After an Emergency Brake Application on Grade — adds requirements on top of the general Rule 112 securement.

stem: Applying enough hand brakes but skipping the effectiveness test means…
choices: securement isn't complete — Rule 112 requires testing, not just applying
it's fine if you used the chart number
answer: securement isn't complete — Rule 112 requires testing, not just applying
explain: Rule 112 isn't satisfied by applying brakes alone — you must test for effectiveness (release air, let slack adjust or apply tractive effort) and add brakes until it holds.

stem: OCS Rules govern movements on…
choices: subdivisions, portions of subdivisions, or other tracks specified in special instructions
answer: subdivisions, portions of subdivisions, or other tracks specified in special instructions
explain: Rule 301(a): movements are governed by OCS Rules on subdivisions/portions/other tracks specified in special instructions.

stem: A clearance is sent direct to the crew. Before it is acted upon…
choices: the conductor and locomotive engineer must both confirm and understand it
only the conductor needs to read it
the RTC reads it aloud once
it is posted at the next station
answer: the conductor and locomotive engineer must both confirm and understand it
explain: Rule 302(b): the clearance is sent direct to the crew; before acting on it the conductor and locomotive engineer must both confirm/understand it.

stem: Where special instructions provide protection against following, the RTC must not authorize a train to follow another until…
choices: the prescribed following-protection conditions are satisfied
the first train clears the subdivision
the following train has a work clearance
answer: the prescribed following-protection conditions are satisfied
explain: Rule 303: where specified, protection against following trains/transfers must be provided before the RTC authorizes a movement to follow a preceding one.

stem: To enter or move within the working limits of a train or transfer, your clearance must restrict you — for example…
choices: 'Protect against Work 5748 between Exeter and Jasper'
answer: 'Protect against Work 5748 between Exeter and Jasper'
explain: Rule 309: a clearance restricts the movement, e.g. 'Protect against Work [number] between [points],' before it may enter or move within those working limits.

stem: Your clearance reads 'Protect against Work 5748 between Exeter and Jasper.' What does that require?
choices: Do not enter or move within Work 5748's limits except as the restriction/foreman permits — protect against it
Stop at Exeter and wait for daylight
answer: Do not enter or move within Work 5748's limits except as the restriction/foreman permits — protect against it
explain: Rule 309: you must protect against the named work — don't enter or move within its limits without the required authority/contact.

stem: A work clearance remains in effect until…
choices: the train reaches the next station
answer: it is superseded or cancelled
explain: Rule 308(b): a work clearance remains in effect until superseded or cancelled, and permits movement in either direction within the named limits.

stem: At a meet, the opposing train's engine and cars have passed you, but you haven't seen its marker. Can you leave the point?
choices: No — it hasn't 'arrived' until its designated engine AND marker have arrived
Yes, if the RTC is busy
answer: No — it hasn't 'arrived' until its designated engine AND marker have arrived
explain: Rule 304: don't leave the named point until the opposing train has arrived — and it hasn't arrived until its designated engine AND marker have arrived (the marker proves the whole train is in).

stem: Before the RTC issues you a clearance through a meet, it must first have…
choices: provided protection against all conflicting movements
answer: provided protection against all conflicting movements
explain: Rule 305: before issuing clearance authority, the RTC must provide protection against all conflicting movements.

stem: In multi-track OCS, your clearance doesn't say which track. Is it valid to proceed?
choices: No — a multi-track OCS clearance must specify the track(s) to be used
Yes, default to the right-hand track
Yes, if you broadcast your track
answer: No — a multi-track OCS clearance must specify the track(s) to be used
explain: Rule 306: in multi-track OCS the clearance must specify the track(s) to be used — without it you don't have valid authority for a track.

stem: In OCS, which rule requires a movement to protect against a foreman working under separate authority?
answer: Rule 311
explain: Rule 311 (Protecting Against a Foreman): the movement's clearance restricts it to protect against the foreman's working limits — you don't enter them without the required protection.

stem: In OCS territory, the RTC supervises by means of…
choices: clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions
answer: clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions
explain: Rule 301(b): the RTC supervises OCS territory by means of clearances, TOP, GBO and other instructions — there are no wayside block signals.

stem: CTC applies within limits specified in…
answer: the timetable or special instructions
explain: Rule 560: CTC is applicable in limits specified in the timetable or special instructions, supervised by the RTC.

stem: Why can the red of a CTC controlled signal be an ABSOLUTE Stop, unlike an automatic block signal?
choices: because the RTC can hold a controlled signal at Stop — it's not a permissive automatic signal
because it has a number plate
it can't — all reds are permissive
answer: because the RTC can hold a controlled signal at Stop — it's not a permissive automatic signal
explain: A controlled signal is held at Stop by the RTC; you need authority (Rule 564) to pass it. An automatic block signal's red is permissive (stop and proceed).

stem: You're stopped at a CTC block signal showing Stop and need to continue. What's required?
choices: authority from the RTC to pass it (Rule 564)
reduce to restricted speed and go
a hand signal from the conductor
answer: authority from the RTC to pass it (Rule 564)
explain: Rule 564: a train/transfer must have authority to pass a block signal indicating Stop; only the RTC can authorize it, after ensuring there are no conflicts.

stem: Before authorizing a movement to pass a CTC stop signal, the RTC must…
choices: ensure no conflicting movements are within/authorized to enter, and block at Stop the controlling devices
simply tell the crew to go
wait for the next train to clear
answer: ensure no conflicting movements are within/authorized to enter, and block at Stop the controlling devices
explain: Rule 564: the RTC ensures there are no conflicting trains/transfers within or authorized to enter, and blocks at Stop the devices controlling signals into those limits, before authorizing the pass.

stem: Two opposing trains are to be lined into the SAME non-signalled siding. Before the signal for either is requested…
choices: each locomotive engineer must be advised of the fact
only the first crew is told
answer: each locomotive engineer must be advised of the fact
explain: Rule 563: when two opposing movements are lined into the same non-signalled siding, each locomotive engineer must be advised before the permitting signal is requested.

stem: Two trains given JOINT work authority in the same limits are each instructed…
choices: to follow one behind the other
answer: 'Protecting against each other'
explain: Rule 567: joint work authority lets more than one movement work the same limits, each instructed 'Protecting against each other.'

stem: You and another crew both hold work authority in the same CTC limits. How do you operate?
choices: protecting against each other within the limits
only one of you may move at a time per the RTC
answer: protecting against each other within the limits
explain: Rule 567 (joint work authority): each movement operates protecting against the other within the specified limits.

stem: A crew authorized to manually operate dual-control switches at a controlled location while switching does so under…
choices: work authority (Rule 566/567), with signal indications suspended while switching
answer: work authority (Rule 566/567), with signal indications suspended while switching
explain: Rule 566.1: such authority is included with work authority (566/567); the indications of those signals are suspended while switching.

stem: CTC is withdrawn from service on your subdivision. You are now governed by…
answer: special instructions
explain: Rule 561: when CTC is withdrawn from service, trains and transfers are governed by special instructions.

stem: Leaving CTC and entering ABS, you must pass a signal indicating Stop. Which rules govern?
choices: Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS
answer: Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS
explain: Rule 565: the movement is governed by Rule 564 within CTC and Rule 509 within ABS.

stem: Interlocking signals for an approaching movement must NOT be restored to Stop unless…
choices: the locomotive engineer has acknowledged they are stopped or able to stop without passing the signal
an emergency exists, with no other conditions
answer: the locomotive engineer has acknowledged they are stopped or able to stop without passing the signal
explain: Rule 604(a): don't restore an approach signal to Stop unless the LE has acknowledged they're stopped or able to stop without passing the signal being restored.

stem: Approaching an automatic interlocking equipped with a timing circuit, you must be prepared to stop if…
choices: you occupy the timing circuit longer than the time specified in special instructions
answer: you occupy the timing circuit longer than the time specified in special instructions
explain: Rule 605: approach prepared to stop if occupying the timing circuit in excess of the time specified in special instructions.

stem: Movements through a MANUAL interlocking are governed by…
answer: special instructions
explain: Rule 608: movements operating through the limits of a manual interlocking are governed by special instructions.

stem: To pass a LOCALLY-CONTROLLED interlocking signal indicating Stop (no conflicting movement)…
choices: you must have authority; the signalman may authorize it after ensuring the route conditions
proceed at restricted speed on your own
a hand signal from the conductor suffices
answer: you must have authority; the signalman may authorize it after ensuring the route conditions
explain: Rule 609: a movement must have authority to pass a locally-controlled interlocking signal at Stop; the signalman may authorize it after the required checks.

stem: To pass a REMOTELY-CONTROLLED interlocking signal indicating Stop, the signalman may authorize it but first must…
choices: ensure there is no conflicting movement in the route
stop all trains on the subdivision
answer: ensure there is no conflicting movement in the route
explain: Rule 610: a movement must have authority; the signalman may authorize the pass only after ensuring no conflicting movement in the route.

stem: At an interlocking signal indicating Stop (no conflicting movement), the rule you follow depends on…
choices: the interlocking TYPE — Manual 608, Locally-Controlled 609, Remotely-Controlled 610, Automatic 611
answer: the interlocking TYPE — Manual 608, Locally-Controlled 609, Remotely-Controlled 610, Automatic 611
explain: Rule 607: the applicable rule is set by the interlocking type (per special instructions) — 608/609/610/611.

stem: Stopped by an AUTOMATIC interlocking signal at Stop: which paragraph of Rule 611 applies if track work is evident?
choices: paragraph (d) — (a),(b),(c) apply only when no other movement or track work is evident
answer: paragraph (d) — (a),(b),(c) apply only when no other movement or track work is evident
explain: Rule 611: (a)/(b)/(c) apply when no other movement or track work is evident; (d) applies when track work IS evident.

stem: You accepted a proceed interlocking signal but stopped before your lead locomotive/car fully passed it. May you continue?
choices: only after receiving permission from the signalman (or per the rule's provisions)
yes, you already had the proceed
yes, if you saw no conflict
answer: only after receiving permission from the signalman (or per the rule's provisions)
explain: Rule 612: if you stop before fully passing a signal you'd accepted, you may proceed only after permission from the signalman or under the rule's provisions.

stem: A single unit of equipment must NOT be left standing…
choices: on the movable portion of an interlocked drawbridge, or within the interlocking limits of a railway crossing at grade
answer: on the movable portion of an interlocked drawbridge, or within the interlocking limits of a railway crossing at grade
explain: Rule 615: don't leave a single unit of equipment standing on a movable drawbridge portion or within the interlocking limits of a railway crossing at grade.

stem: There's no advance signal before the interlocking. How must you approach the limits?
choices: prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop
at track speed, expecting a clear route
answer: prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop
explain: Rule 606: where there's no advance signal, approach interlocking limits prepared to comply with a signal indicating Stop.

stem: The interlocking signals can display the proper indication for your move. May the signalman use a radio or hand signal instead?
choices: No — radio/hand signals must not be used when the proper indication can be displayed
Yes, if the radio is clearer
answer: No — radio/hand signals must not be used when the proper indication can be displayed
explain: Rule 602(a): except in emergency, radio or hand signals must not be used when the proper indication can be displayed by the interlocking signals.

stem: A track unit must not operate beyond the governing stop sign at a non-interlocked drawbridge or crossing until…
choices: it has been ascertained that the route is properly lined
answer: it has been ascertained that the route is properly lined
explain: Rule 620(b): operation beyond the governing stop sign must not be made until it's ascertained the route is properly lined (special instructions govern when an attendant is in charge).

stem: A derailment or damage to an interlocking is known or suspected. The rule that applies is…
answer: Rule 616
explain: Rule 616 (Damage to Interlocking) governs the response when a derailment or damage to the interlocking is known or suspected.

stem: Within interlocking limits, instructions may be issued when necessary by…
answer: a signalman
explain: Rule 601: within interlocking limits, interlocking signal indications govern the routes, and a signalman may issue instructions when necessary.

stem: A GBO/clearance/other authority addressed to a movement is regarded as addressed to…
choices: the conductor and locomotive engineer (and the pilot or snow-plow foreman, if any)
answer: the conductor and locomotive engineer (and the pilot or snow-plow foreman, if any)
explain: Rule 135: an authority addressed to a movement is regarded as addressed to the conductor and locomotive engineer, and also the pilot or snow-plow foreman if any.

stem: A crew member who copies a GBO or clearance must also ensure…
choices: that those addressed receive a copy
it is filed with the RTC
answer: that those addressed receive a copy
explain: Rule 135: the crew member copying a GBO/clearance must ensure those addressed receive a copy.

stem: Every conductor, locomotive engineer and pilot must read and properly understand all GBO and clearances…
choices: as soon as possible after receiving them, and make them available to other crew members
answer: as soon as possible after receiving them, and make them available to other crew members
explain: Rule 142: read and have a proper understanding of all GBO/clearances as soon as possible, and make them available to other crew members as soon as practicable.

stem: On a track where a DOB applies, a movement must not move unless in possession of…
choices: the current DOB (or an applicable TGBO)
answer: the current DOB (or an applicable TGBO)
explain: Rule 156: don't move where a DOB is applicable unless in possession of the current DOB (or a TGBO applicable within those limits).

stem: On a track where a TGBO applies, a movement must not move unless…
choices: in possession of a TGBO addressed to them
the RTC says so by radio
answer: in possession of a TGBO addressed to them
explain: Rule 157: don't move where a TGBO is applicable unless in possession of a TGBO addressed to the movement.

stem: The GBO numbers in effect for a movement are listed…
answer: on its clearance
explain: Rule 143: GBO numbers applicable to a movement are shown on its clearance, so the crew knows which bulletins govern.

stem: The RTC's written record of a GBO/clearance/TOP/authority must be made…
choices: prior to or during the transmission
at the end of the shift
answer: prior to or during the transmission
explain: Rule 131: the RTC maintains a complete record (book or computer-assisted system) made prior to or during the transmission.

stem: You're copying a clearance from the RTC by radio. How do you copy it?
choices: copy as it's transmitted, then repeat back from the copy received all applicable written and pre-printed portions, spelling station names
copy only the parts that apply to you
record it after the call ends
answer: copy as it's transmitted, then repeat back from the copy received all applicable written and pre-printed portions, spelling station names
explain: Rule 136(a): copy as transmitted and repeat back from the copy received all applicable written/pre-printed portions, spelling out station names.

stem: A written authority must be in the prescribed form and…
choices: without erasure or any condition that makes it hard to read
answer: without erasure or any condition that makes it hard to read
explain: Rule 132: brief but clear, in the prescribed form, without erasure or any condition that may render it difficult to read.

stem: Each RTC desk in a multi-desk office uses a separate number series for GBO/clearance/TOP because…
choices: it prevents confusion between desks and adjacent territories
answer: it prevents confusion between desks and adjacent territories
explain: Rule 133: separate series per desk (except where computer-controlled) keeps numbering from colliding between desks and adjacent territories.

stem: You have a CLEAR ABS block signal. Does it authorize you to occupy the main track?
choices: No — block signals govern the blocks but do not authorize main track occupancy
Only with the RTC's word too
answer: No — block signals govern the blocks but do not authorize main track occupancy
explain: Rule 505: block signals govern the use of the blocks; they don't authorize main track occupancy or dispense with other required signals.

stem: When are RTC instructions NOT required to pass an ABS signal indicating Stop?
choices: when the movement is required to operate at restricted speed
when no train is in sight
answer: when the movement is required to operate at restricted speed
explain: Rule 509 EXCEPTION: instructions aren't required when a movement is already required to operate at restricted speed.

stem: Entering a main track in ABS with no movement observed approaching, you reverse the switch — then…
choices: wait five minutes (unless more is specified), remaining at the switch, before moving foul
call the RTC for a clearance
answer: wait five minutes (unless more is specified), remaining at the switch, before moving foul
explain: Rule 513(a): reverse the switch and wait five minutes (unless a greater period is specified), remaining at the switch, before moving foul of the main track.

stem: Delayed in an ABS block you entered on a signal permitting better than RESTRICTED speed, you move to the next signal at REDUCED speed UNLESS…
choices: there are no switches between you and the next signal, or until the track is seen to be clear
answer: there are no switches between you and the next signal, or until the track is seen to be clear
explain: Rule 515: move at REDUCED speed to the next signal — unless there are no switches between, or until the track is seen to be clear.

stem: ABS signals on your line are withdrawn from service. You're now governed by…
choices: instructions from the RTC or special instructions
answer: instructions from the RTC or special instructions
explain: Rule 507: when ABS signals are withdrawn from service, movements are governed by RTC instructions or special instructions.

stem: All crew members are responsible for…
choices: the safe operation of movements and equipment in their charge and the observance of the rules
answer: the safe operation of movements and equipment in their charge and the observance of the rules
explain: Rule 106: all crew members are responsible for the safe operation of movements/equipment in their charge and for the observance of the rules.

stem: On a standing movement, the locomotive engineer must temporarily leave the controlling cab. Before stepping off, they must…
choices: fully apply the independent brake, apply the automatic brake if required, and remove the reverser (unless excepted)
just set the throttle to idle
answer: fully apply the independent brake, apply the automatic brake if required, and remove the reverser (unless excepted)
explain: Rule 109: leaving the cab on a standing movement, the LE must fully apply the independent brake, apply the automatic brake if required, and remove the reverser (unless an exception applies).

stem: Why does Rule 109 require the locomotive engineer to remove the reverser when leaving the cab?
choices: to prevent the locomotive from being powered or moved while unattended at the controls
it doesn't — that step is optional
answer: to prevent the locomotive from being powered or moved while unattended at the controls
explain: Rule 109: removing the reverser (unless excepted) prevents the locomotive from being powered/moved while the engineer is away from the controls.
